Conseil du ministre
[1] XXXXXXX (hereinafter referred to as the "male claimant") and his spouse, XXXXXXXX XXXXX (hereinafter referred to as the "female claimant"), along with their children, XXXXXXXXXX XXXX., and XXXXXX (hereinafter referred to as "the children"), are citizens of Hungary claiming refugee protection under sections 96 and 97(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act (IRPA).1 These claims were joined according to Rule 49(1) of the Refugee Protection Division Rules. The father of the children was appointed as their Designated Representative according to section 167(2) of the IRPA. The identities of the claimants as nationals of Hungary are established by true certified copies of their Hungarian passports.2 The hearing into this claim was adjourned in order to give counsel for the claimants an opportunity to prepare written submissions. The Board received counsel's written submission on February 8, 2012.
ALLEGATIONS
[2] A summary of the female claimant's narrative is as follows:
[3] The principal female claimant fears persecution for herself and her family on account of her Roma ethnicity. She is married to an ethnic Hungarian. The principal female claimant was segregated in school. She was hit by her teachers and other non-Roma students. She completed elementary school and then went to trade school. She dropped out because she was harassed. She switched her trade but again she dropped out. At times, she was beaten up by her schoolmates and other ethnic Hungarians. She was only able to find work through Roma friends. The principal female claimant's husband was abused at work because of her Roma ethnicity. The principal female claimant did not report the abuse to the police because she learned through experience that they did not care about the Roma. In 2001, a neighbour attacked the female claimant's brother with a hammer because he was Roma. The attacker called the police and admitted that he attacked her brother but the police refused to charge him. In 2004, strangers broke into the female claimant's house. They threw her off the terrace and she was injured. She did not report it to the police because she did not believe that they would have helped her. In 2005, the female claimant's brother had his house firebombed by racists who owed him money. The ambulance and the police were called and police told him that they did not catch the culprit. Her brother told the police what happened and they said they don't care what happens to gypsies. In 2006, the female claimant's husband was beaten up. The attacker hit her husband and he injured his head and ear. It was reported to the police but the police only sent the attacker a letter. In 2007, the female claimant's older son was attacked by members of the Hungarian Guard. The female claimant's brother was attacked in 2008. His attackers threatened to kill him. When the police showed up, the attackers had fled. The police told him it made no sense to file a report. In XXXX of 2009, the female claimant and her husband started getting telephone calls threatening to cut her throat and that of her husband and children. In XXXX of 2009, when her husband was away, members of the Guard broke into the female claimant's home, assaulted her, and wanted to know where her husband was. When her son came to her aid he was beaten also. In XXXXXX of 2009, the female claimant and her family fled Hungary and came to Canada where they claimed refugee status.
DETERMINATION
[4] I find that the female claimant has not satisfied the burden of establishing a serious possibility of persecution for a Convention ground, or that she would personally be subjected, on a balance of probabilities, to a danger of torture, or a risk to life or a risk of cruel and unusual treatment or punishment upon return to Hungary. Therefore, I find that the female claimant is neither a Convention refugee nor a person in need of protection pursuant to sections 96 and 97(1) of the IRPA.
[5] The determinative issue in this case is whether the female claimant's fear is objectively reasonable. In this regard, I considered whether or not there is adequate state protection in Hungary, whether the female claimant took all reasonable steps to avail herself of that protection, and whether she has provided clear and convincing evidence of the state's inability to protect her.
ANALYSIS
Section 96 - Convention Refugee Grounds
State Protection
[6] In assessing the issue of state protection, I am guided by a number of cases. The principles were applied to this case. There is a presumption, except in situations where the state is in a complete breakdown, that it is capable of protecting its citizens. A claimant, who alleges that state protection is inadequate, must persuade the Board that, on a balance of probabilities, the evidence establishes that the state protection is inadequate.3 A claimant can rebut the presumption by providing clear and convincing evidence of the state's inability to protect. For example, a claimant might advance testimony of similarly-situated individuals let down by the state protection arrangement or the claimant's testimony of past personal incidents in which state protection did not materialize. The onus is on the claimant to approach the state for protection in situations where state protection might be reasonably forthcoming.4 While the effectiveness of the protection is a relevant consideration,5 recent Federal Court decisions have held that the test for a finding of state protection is whether the protection is adequate, rather than the effectiveness per se.6 The protection need not be perfect.7 One accepted measure of assessment is whether the state is making "serious efforts" to protect its citizens.8 This standard continues to be applied in many Federal Court decisions.9 As stated above, no government is expected to guarantee perfect protection to all of its citizens at all times, and the fact that a state, in not always successful in protecting its citizens, is not enough to justify a claim, especially where a state is in effective control of its territory, has military, police and civil authorities in place and is making serious efforts to protect its citizens.10 Less than perfect protection is not a basis to determine that a state is either unwilling or unable to offer reasonable protection.11
[7] Furthermore, the law states that the burden to prove an absence of state protection is directly proportional to the level of democracy of that state.12 Hungary is a democracy. Therefore, the presumption of state protection is a strong one. The claimant must do more than merely show that he or she went to see members of the police force and that those efforts were unsuccessful. The more democratic the state's institutions, the greater the onus on the claimant to show he or she has exhausted all courses of action available.13 Moreover, local failures by the authorities to provide effective protection do not mean that the state, as a whole, fails to protect its citizens, unless the failures form part of a broader pattern of state inability or refusal to provide protection.14 Indeed, if a claimant believes the actions of some police officers are corrupt, the onus is on them to approach other members of the security forces or authorities.15 Where there is evidence that the government is taking steps to protect the target group, in the absence of evidence to the contrary, it must be presumed that these steps will be effective.16 It is not enough that a government is willing to provide protection and is making serious efforts to do so. In order for state protection to be present, the efforts made must adequately protect citizens in practice.17 In order for adequate state protection to exist, a government must have both the will and capacity to implement effectively its legislation and programmes.18
[8] Hungary is a republic with a population of approximately 10 million and a multiparty parliamentary democracy. The National Assembly elections on April 11 and 25 were assessed as free and fair, with the conservative Fidesz-Christian Democrat (KDNP) coalition winning enough seats in the second round to achieve a two-thirds majority.19
[9] Hungary is in effective control of its territory and has in place a functioning security force to uphold the laws and constitution of the country. There is nothing in the documentation before the Board to suggest that Hungary is in a state of complete breakdown.
[10] According to the Hungarian Prison Service, the prison population increased to 132 percent of capacity as of December 31, compared with 129 percent in 2009. On December 31, 16,366 inmates were in prisons and detention centres. On July 19, the government reopened the prison in Solt to reduce overcrowding, increasing the capacity of the system by 288.20
[11] The National Police Headquarters (NPH), which operates under the direction of the Ministry of Interior, is responsible for enforcing laws and maintaining order nationwide. Twenty regional police departments are directly subordinate to the NPH; city police are subordinate to the regional police and have local jurisdiction. Penalties for police officers found guilty of wrongdoing include reprimand, dismissal, and criminal prosecution. A legislative amendment that took effect on March 24 mandates the automatic dismissal of police officers convicted of criminal acts committed while on duty. Victims of lesser police abuses may complain either to the alleged violator's unit or to the Independent Police Complaints Board (IPCB). In the first 10 months of the year, authorities found 2,914 police officers responsible for breaches of discipline, 868 guilty of petty offences, 310 guilty of criminal offenses, and eight unfit for duty. In the same period, courts sentenced seven police officers to prison terms, gave suspended sentences to 25, fined 315, demoted four, and dismissed 11. In the same period courts convicted 32 officers of corruption. In December 2009 the prosecutor's office in Eger opened an investigation into an allegation by a Romani man that five police officers assaulted him. The case remained pending at year's end. Research conducted in 2008 by the HHC with data from the NHP and the National Police College indicated that Roma were three times more likely to be stopped for identification checks than non-Roma.21 The Hungarian Civil Liberties Union (HCLU) reported that police often verbally and physically abused suspects of Roma origin in certain areas of the country, most frequently in the northeastern Borsod-Abauj-Zemplen County.22
[12] The law provides criminal penalties for official corruption; however, the government did not implement the law effectively, and NGOs contended that officials often engaged in corrupt practices with impunity. Corruption in the executive and legislative branches reportedly increased during the year, and numerous cases of alleged corruption received significant public attention. According to the World Bank's Worldwide Governance Indicators, government corruption was a problem. Penalties for police officers found guilty of wrongdoing include reprimand, dismissal, and criminal prosecution. Several government offices were responsible for combating corruption. The State Audit Office audited the public sector and campaign spending of political parties. The independent judiciary, the prosecutors, the police, and in certain cases the customs and finance guard were responsible for investigating corruption.23
[13] With respect to police corruption and misconduct and recourse available to citizens, documentary evidence indicates that the Independent Police Complaints Board (IPCB) began operation in January 2008. The IPCB, which is independent of the police, reviews complaints of police actions which violate fundamental rights. Sources report that the IPCB is composed of five members who make recommendations to the head of the National Police. If the recommendations are not accepted, the matter can be referred to a court. A report by the COE's European Committee for the Prevention of Torture and Inhuman or Degrading Treatment or Punishment (CPT) states that, according to the IPCB members, the police followed up on only a small proportion of the IPCB's recommendations. According to a report evaluating the first year and a half of the IPCB, HHC notes that the IPCB's investigative rights are "insufficient" and are usually limited to the complaint and the file of the case submitted by the police, making it difficult for the member to reconstruct the facts. Country Reports 2009 indicates that by 15 December 2009, the IPCB had received 697 complaints from the public, reviewed 457 complaints and found violations in 215 cases; of 52 cases forwarded to the National Police Chief, the Chief agreed with 8 cases, partially accepted the findings of 23 cases, and rejected the findings of the other 21 cases. Another avenue of redress is the Parliamentary Commissioners' Office (also known as the Ombudsmen). There are four Parliamentary Commissioners: the Parliamentary Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information, the Parliamentary Commissioner for the National and Ethnic Minorities Rights, the Parliamentary Commissioner for Future Generations, and the Parliamentary Commissioner for Civil Rights. The General Guide to the Parliamentary Commissioner's Office states that anyone whose constitutional rights were violated, or who has received threats of violations, by a public employee or official (including police), could apply to the Ombudsman for Civil Rights. Examples of grievances listed in the guide are: "unreasonably long procedures; discrimination; inaccurate, or wrong information provided; inequitable personal treatment; unreasonable refusal of information dissemination; unlawful decision; and other omission". The ombudsmen are elected by Parliament to six-year terms and are responsible solely to Parliament. Although the recommendations of the ombudsman institutions have no binding effect, the proportion of recommendations accepted is high. Country Reports 2009 states that the ombudsmen operate without government interference and that the public is generally positive about their activities. There is also a complaints office at the National Police headquarters. Police action, or inaction, can be challenged in the civil courts; victims can file lawsuits for damages to remedy personal rights violations. There is no single independent institution in Hungary to investigate corruption cases, but there are a number of governmental departments mandated to fight different types of corruption. The Defence Service of Law Enforcement Agencies, which falls under the Ministry of Justice and Law Enforcement, investigates if a member of law enforcement is involved in a corruption case; citizens can turn to them with information. High-level corruption and organized crime cases are under the jurisdiction of the Central Investigation Department of the National Office of the Prosecutor.24 The ECRI, in its report covering the year of 2008, notes that incidents of police brutality towards Roma continue to be reported. It also notes that Prejudice against Roma still permeates the police force, as other sectors of society. According to this report, at the end of 2006, a number of anti-Roma postings were reported by two police officers to have been made on an internal web site of the national police. This web site was immediately suspended and an investigation launched. Several police officers found responsible for the postings were later sent to tolerance training.25
[14] With respect to anti-discrimination remedies available to Romas, documentary evidence indicates that the Hungarian government has taken a number of legal and institutional measures to improve the situation of the Romani minority. According to the Open Society Institute (OSI), "Hungary has one of the most advanced systems for minority protection in the region". In 2008, Hungary allocated a budget of approximately 5.2 billion forints to various ministries and local governments for programs supporting Romani integration, compared with 4.5 billion forints in 2007. However, a May 2008 report published by the state audit office indicated that despite a significant increase in government funding to Romani programs, resources "routinely failed to reach the groups with the greatest needs". The Hungarian Government lists a number of institutions within the state apparatus, including the Roma Integration Department within the Ministry of Social Affairs and Labour, the main government body responsible for Romani integration and the Council of Roma Integration, a "consultative, advisory body," which coordinates measures between various ministries on the social inclusion of Roma. The Inter-ministerial Committee on Roma Affairs is a government body headed by the leader of the Romani self-government that "coordinate[s] state tasks pertaining to Roma issues". With regard to victims of discrimination, the offended party can turn to the Parliamentary Commissioner for the Rights of National and Ethnic Minorities. The Minorities Commissioner (or Minorities Ombudsman) aims to investigate or to have investigated any violations which come to his attention concerning national and ethnic rights, and to initiate general or individual measures in order to remedy them. The Minorities Commissioner examines whether the processes of the authorities and public service providers (for example, local governments, the police, gas works, and billing companies) comply with the requirements of constitutionality and legality. The Minorities Commissioner may also take action if he becomes aware of unjust procedures or negative discrimination. The Minorities Commissioner helps in enforcing the rights of complainants by (a) informing them about the applicable rules of law, and the available possibilities of legal remedy, (b) issuing interpreting provisions on minority rights (legally non-binding standpoints) and (c) through mediation. According to the Minority Rights Group International (MRGI), the Minorities Commissioner "plays an active role in the examination of allegations of discrimination against the Roma community and promotes a uniform anti-discrimination law". However, the HHC criticizes what it considers the limited scope of corrective action that can be taken by the Minorities Ombudsman in cases of abuse. The Equal Treatment Authority (ETA) is an independent organization established by the Hungarian government that is tasked with investigating complaints of discrimination against public bodies such as the police, military, municipalities, and schools in the fields of employment, education, access to goods or any other field. Between 2005 and 2006, approximately 30 percent of cases handled by the ETA concerned Roma. The ETA may take various actions against an offending body, such as ordering it to cease its discriminatory practice, publicizing the result of an investigation or imposing a fine of up to 6,000,000 Hungarian forints. The ETA found seven occurrences of ethnic discrimination in 2007, four in 2008 and one in the first half of 2009 (with an agreement being reached by parties in two other cases). For example, on 19 December 2007 the ETA imposed a 600,000 forint fine on a pizzeria that had placed a sign outside its doors stating "We don't serve Gypsies". Other possible avenues for recourse in cases of discrimination include: the Parliamentary Commissioner for Civil Rights (PCCR), which investigates violations of constitutional rights by public bodies; the Independent Police Complaints Commission (IPCC); the Hungarian Labour Inspectorate; the National Consumer Protection Authority; the Commissioner for Educational Rights; the Health Insurance Supervisory Authority; the Patients' Rights Representatives; and the Central Office of Justice, which provides "counselling, legal aid and financial help for victims of crimes, in particular those who suffered bodily or emotional harm and mental shock". In 2008, the PCCR had a caseload of 1,033, of which 501 involved cases from the Romani minority. Victims of discrimination may also take their case to various courts, such as the Labour Court in employment cases. According to information provided by the Embassy of Hungary: Damage can only be awarded by the court. To get compensation the victim has to sue the violator at the court. In case of labour discrimination, the offended person can initiate a court proceeding at the Labour Court. In other cases, the discriminative act can violate the personal rights of the individual which falls under the authority of the Civil Court. According to the Criminal Code 'any person who assaults another person for being part, whether in fact or under presumption, of a national, ethnic, racial or religious group, or of a certain societal group, or compels him by applying coercion or duress to do, not to do, or to endure something, is guilty of a felony and' can be punished by the Criminal Court by imprisonment for up to five years. Unlike the ETA, courts may offer damages to victims; however, the procedures are reportedly "very long and the willingness of courts to acknowledge non-material damage is very low". Since 2001, attorneys at the Roma Anti-Discrimination Network Service (run jointly by the Ministry of Justice and Law Enforcement and the National Roma Self-Government) have been providing free legal aid to Romani clients in discrimination cases. As of June 2009, there were between 30 and 44 attorneys working in the Network, an increase from 23 in 2001. In 2007; however, the United Nations (UN) Independent Expert on Minority Issues wrote that "it is likely that due to lack of information and inadequate coverage of an effective social worker system in disadvantaged areas and isolated settlements, a relatively high percentage of Roma are failing to apply for full benefits to which they are entitled." For example, a survey by the European Union Minorities and Discrimination Survey (EU-MIDIS) found that 78 percent of Romani respondents from Hungary were unaware of any organization that offered support services for people who faced discrimination. NGOs such as the Roma Civil Rights Foundation (RPA), the Legal Defence Bureau for National and Ethnic Minorities (NEKI) and the Legal Counselling Office of the Roma Parliament have a better reputation than government organizations for assisting Romani victims of discrimination. NGOs provide anti-discrimination training for members of the judiciary. According to the HHC, organizations like NEKI and the RPA have "limited financial and human resources," which restricts the number of cases that they can take per year. As of June 2009, a coalition of NGOs funded by the OSI was reportedly in the process of establishing a legal aid program for victims of hate crimes.26
[15] Further documentary evidence indicates that the mandate of the Roma Police Officers' Association is to secure and protect the rights of the members related to their employment rights and interests, especially anti-discrimination and equal opportunities. They are also required to provide indirect representation (mediation) in cases where a violation of the above-mentioned rights has occurred, especially when the right to equal treatment is violated by a discriminatory measure. Even in the case of a non-member, the association still decided on the merits of the case and may decided to take up the case and contact a legal representative/organizations. The training and education of young Roma and other minorities for employment in the police/military and promotes equal treatment in the law enforcement authorities, provides help to Roma with identity problems, participates in cultural activities, provides/organises educational events, arranges workshops and works for the protection of human rights. Roma are able to file a complaint with the association. The complaints mostly concern discrimination in employment, discriminatory treatment, and discrimination by law enforcement authorities or police offices. The Roma Police Officers' Association is distinct from FAERLEO (Fraternal Association of European Roma Law Enforcement Officers).27
[16] In its report covering 2008, the ECRI notes that, as regards the Roma minority, their situation of disadvantage is such that long-term but intensive efforts will be needed to turn it around; while many of the measures taken to date may have a positive impact, they need to be continued and in some cases intensified in order to achieve lasting results. In the field of employment, many initiatives have been taken, often with a twin aim of assisting in finding employment and developing new skills, but these measures are often short-term and can only help a small number of Roma at a time. In practice, Roma continue to face both a disproportionately high rate of unemployment, and discrimination in access to employment. In the fields of education and housing, the efforts of the central authorities are frequently hampered by the manner in which local authorities translate the measures taken into practice; numerous abuses have been reported, and the central authorities appear somewhat hamstrung in their efforts to achieve change. Roma families are deprived of access to social housing by discriminatory rules and practices of local authorities; and Roma children are still confronted with segregation in schools.28
[17] Between 2008 and 2009, there was a wave of violence directed against the Roma in Hungary. In October of 2009, the Council of Europe's Commissioner for Human Rights expressed concerns about the rise of extremism, and appeal to all political party leaders to ensure that no xenophobic or anti-Roma statements be made in the 2010 parliamentary election campaign. In 2009, violent attacks against Roma continued. The Hungarian National Bureau of Investigation, a police agency investigating serious crimes, strengthened a special task force to 120 officers to investigate a series of attacks against the Romani community. XXXXXXXX and his son, aged five, were killed in XXXXXXXXXX in February. After an initial examination, the local police announced that they had been found dead after a fire caused by an electrical fault in their house. Later that same day, however, the police acknowledged that evidence of gunshot wounds had been found on the bodies, but only opened a murder investigation 10 hours later. In XXXX, the Minister of Justice stated that a disciplinary procedure against local police officers had been launched. In XXXXXX, the Independent Police Complaints Commission examining the police investigation of the killings in XXXXXXXXXX concluded that the local police had seriously violated the fundamental rights of the victims of the attack to an effective investigation. XXXXXX, a 54-year-old-Romani man, was killed in XXXXXX Roma neighbourhood in April. He was reportedly shot dead as he left his home to start the night shift in the local XXXXXXXXX where he worked. The police stated there were similarities between XXXXXXX case and the earlier attacks against the Romani community. XXXXXXXX, a 45-year-old Romani woman, was shot dead and her 13-year-old daughter seriously injured in the village of XXXX in August. Later that month, police detained four men suspected of this killing and at least five other deadly attacks on Romani people, including the killing of XXXXXXXX and his son, and XXXXXX. All four suspected denied involvement in the attacks and were being held in pre-trial detention at the end of the year. The Chief of National Police said in XXXX that they had evidence linking the suspects to acts of deadly violence against the Romani community between XXXXXX 2008 and XXXX 2009, and that racism appeared to have been the main motive.29 In XXXXXX 2007, the public prosecutor filed a suit to disband the Hungarian Guard. The procedure was slowed down by the fact that in XXXX of 2008 the judge in charge returned the case, after being threatened by telephone. The ruling was given in XXXXXX of 2008. One of the specific events prompting the ruling to disband the Guard was the march staged by the organization in XXXXXXXXXX in Pest County in XXXXXX 2007. The speeches made there focused on "Gypsy crime", thereby violating the dignity of the Gypsy minority. According to the court inciting anger towards the Gypsy minority is unacceptable in a constitutional framework. The Roma community and people supporting constitutional values who show solidarity with them hoped that following the court ruling there would be an end to the organized recruiting of members, and public events. However, the actual situation is that the former members-based on the provisions of Act III of 1989 on the Right to Assembly-can continue to hold demonstrations. The police can only ban these in advance based on the legislation in force if the event being held at the place or time indicated in the announcement would seriously put at risk the proper operation of the bodies of popular representation or the courts, or if traffic circulation is not possible on another route. The police can only break up the event if exercise of the right to assembly constitutes a crime to commit a crime and involves the violation of the rights and freedom of others or the participants are carrying weapons. Theoretically, it is possible for a speaker from the Guard to be guilty of the crime of incitement against a community by calling for violence against Roma people. In reality-bearing in mind the relevant practice of the Constitutional Court-it is extremely rare for anyone to be found guilty of this crime, since the court would virtually have to show a causal connection between the speech and the occurrence of violence.30 Since 2008 Amnesty International has received reports of a number of violent attacks against Roma and their properties in Hungary. These reports described, among others, a series of nine attacks against members of the Romani communities across Hungary which occurred in 2008 and 2009 and which claimed six lives. Amnesty International's research into some of the nine attacks and other reported incidents indicates that the Hungarian authorities failed to identify and respond effectively to violence against Roma in Hungary, including by not investigating possible racial motivation. The assistance and support provided by the state for victims of hate crimes are also inadequate. In its report on the violent incidents against Roma in Hungary published in June 2010, the OSCE noted that Roma were more susceptible to become "scapegoats" and blamed for the country's existing socioeconomic problems, as larger proportion of them depended on state support. An increase in the number of racist attacks was acknowledged by the Deputy Minister of the Ministry of Justice and Law Enforcement, Andras Turi.31
[18] Human rights problems during 2010 included extremist violence and harsh rhetoric against ethnic and religious minority groups and discrimination against Roma in education, housing, employment, and access to social services. Violent attacks against Roma continued, generating strong public concern and intense disputes as to the existence and scale of racially motivated crimes. Human rights NGOs complained that law enforcement authorities were reluctant to recognize racial motivation for many crimes. On XXXXXX, Molotov cocktails were thrown at the houses of four Romani families in the town of XXXX, one of which suffered a serious fire. No one was injured. Police spokesman XXXXXX confirmed that the incident had taken place, but gave no details, stating that an investigation was ongoing. On XXXXX, one of the deputies of the National Roma Minority Self-Government was attacked in the town of XXX. According to the victim's media statements, two "active members of an extremist party and organization" were demolishing the pub he owned and as he approached, they kicked him and hit him. Police were not able to identify any perpetrators. On XXXXX, unknown persons threw several bottles filled with gasoline through the window of a house inhabited by Roma in XXXX. Damage was estimated at 5,000 forint ($24), and no injuries were reported. Police were unable to identify any perpetrators. On XXXX, unknown persons attacked a Romani house in XXXXXX. They fired three shots at the house, leaving bullet holes in the front wall. When the attack occurred, a mother and her children were sleeping in the house. There were no reports of injuries. Police could not identify the perpetrators. On August 9, the National Bureau of Investigation announced the completion of its investigation into a series of physical attacks against Roma in 2008 and 2009. As a result of the investigation, the Pest County Prosecutor charged four suspects with the murder of six persons, the serious injury of five others, and threats to the safety of an additional 55. The case was scheduled to go to trial in 2011. Human rights NGOs reported that Roma were discriminated against in almost all fields of life, particularly in employment, education, housing, penal institutions, and access to public places, such as restaurants and bars.32
[19] A particularly alarming development has occurred in Hungary since ECRI's third report, in the form of a sharp rise in racism in public discourse in the year of 2008. Anti-Semitic articles regularly appear in the press and on the internet, and anti-Roma discourse appears to be becoming increasingly virulent and wide-spread. The creation and increasing visibility of one radical right-wing group in particular has led to grave concerns amongst members of civil society and the government, due not only to the group's openly anti-Roma and anti-Semitic discourse but also to its paramilitary-style uniforms and insignia that are strongly reminiscent of a right-wing party that briefly held power in Hungary during the second World War, and during whose term in power tens of thousands of Jews and Roma were killed. At least one act of racist violence appears to have been linked to the racist discourse of this group. Since its creation in August of 2007, the Hungarian Guard has organized numerous public rallies throughout the country, including in villages with large Roma populations, despite apparently innocuous articles of association, amongst the group's chief messages is the defence of ethnic Hungarians against so-called "Gypsy crime". Other extremist marches and rallies have also been held in recent months, along with increasingly strong counter-demonstrations. In March, a neo-Nazi rally outside a Budapest ticket office attracted around 1,000 demonstrators; close by, around 3000 people, including the Prime Minister, held a counter-demonstration. Isolated incidents of particularly severe racist violence have, however, been reported by the media, and further incidents reported by numerous actors in civil society-including some incidents of police brutality against Roma, and one incident in which, only a few days after the group had held a march there, two Roma women in a small town were beaten by two sympathizers of the Hungarian Guard, who openly stated that they assaulted the women because they were Roma. ECRI notes with concern that such anecdotal evidence does not provide a reliable basis for building up a true picture of the prevalence or otherwise of racist violence in Hungary, or for taking effective preventive action against such violence when it occurs. NGOs emphasize in this respect that a rarity of reports of racist violence is not in itself an indication that such acts are not committed, as victims of such acts may often be reluctant to come forward at all or to report the racist elements of violent offences against the person, whether owing to a sense of shame, due to fear of retribution, or because they feel it is unlikely that serious follow-up will be given to this aspect of a crime.33
[20] With regard to the effectiveness and the level of response by law enforcement to racially motivated threats of violence and criminal violence directed against members of the Roma community, according to one source, three non-governmental organizations (NGOs), law enforcement authorities systematically fail to provide effective protection to the Roma. The European Roma Rights Centre (ERRC), the Chance for Children Foundation (CFCF), a Hungarian organization founded in 2003 to combat discrimination against Roma and socially-deprived children in public education, and the Hungarian Helsinki Committee (HHC), a non-profit organization that monitors the enforcement of human rights, are of the view, that, even though Hungary has one of the most advanced anti-discrimination laws and a system for minority protection in the Central and Eastern European region, and has developed mechanisms to ensure that minority groups enjoy civil and political rights, the central government's failure to maintain strong and effective control mechanisms over rights violations takes its toll on Hungary's largest minority, the Roma. Another group submission to the UN-this one by, among others, the CFCF, ERRC, the Hungarian Civil Liberties Union and the Hungarian Association for Persons with Intellectual Disability-says that according to NGOs, Roma victims face very often discriminatory treatment by the police; officers are reluctant to register their reports and especially the racial motivation of a crime reported. A September 2010 shadow report, prepared for the UN Human Rights Committee by the Legal Defence Bureau for National and Ethnic Minorities (NEKI), Minority Groups Group International (MRG) and the Serbian Institute of Budapest, also states that complaints submitted to NEKI show that the Roma minority still experiences discrimination by the police during investigations and criminal proceedings. Roma victims often experience discriminatory treatment (lack of information and legal help, reluctance from the police etc.) by the police from the very first moment when they want to report a crime. This problem is the most tangible in hate crime investigations, where Roma are the victims of serious racially-motivated crimes. Human Rights First notes in its 2010 report on ant-Roma violence in Hungary that authorities such as the police showed efforts in bringing perpetrators of "high-profile" crimes to justice, especially crimes reported in the media. In one example, two policemen were held accountable for their "misconduct" in their initial response to the investigation of a hate-motivated double murder committed in 2009. However, the organization further indicates that the "authorities" have a "poor record" of justice when working on the "other serious cases of violence" that have been documented by human rights groups.34
[21] According to the documentary evidence, where a Roma victim of violence or threats has made a complaint to the local police which has not been handled adequately or fairly, there is recourse to different complaint mechanisms. According to the Minorities Ombudsman, the Police Act of 1994 allows for a complaint against an "unlawful action" of a police officer to be submitted to the local captain of the police, with a higher ranking captain having the option to appeal the decision. The resulting decision can be reviewed by the court. The HHC stated that complaints shall be submitted within 30 days of the alleged violation by the concerned person or his or her representative. Complaints may also be represented by human rights NGOs or foundations, minority self-governments or certain law professors. The complaint is decided in an administrative procedure by the head of the respective police unit within 30 days after receiving the complaint. The HHC noted that this "first instance decision" can be appealed "on any ground" to the "superior of the concerned police unit". If the appeal is forwarded to it, the superior body shall deliver a reasoned decision, affirming, modifying or annulling the first instance decision. The subsequent "second instance" decision may be challenged before the court by the complainant. However, the Court "may not change the decision: it may only send the case back to the police and order a new procedure. The police are obliged to follow the guidelines set by the court in the new procedure.35
[22] A Hungarian government report to the UN Human Rights Council states that the Independent Police Complaints Body, which is based in Budapest, has been responsible for "performing civil control over Police activities" since September 1, 2007. The Complaints Body is mainly involved in investigating "complaints received in connection with police actions and omissions. The HHC also notes that, since January 1, 2008, the Complaints Body investigates violations and omissions committed by the Police, provided that such violations and omissions substantively concern fundamental rights. The Minorities Ombudsman specified that the Complaints Body receives complaints against the police for such things as discrimination and "racial brutality". According to Hungary's report to the UN, the National Police Commissioner is required to submit monthly reports to the Complaints Body about complaints submitted to the police. If the Complaints Body finds that a citizen's fundamental rights have been seriously violated, it sends its decision to the Police Commissioner who shall deliver a resolution within 30 days. Although the Police Commissioner delivers the decision on the individual complaint, he can only deviate from the Complaint Body's recommendation on "the basis of detailed argumentation. The Police Commissioner's decision can be submitted to court for judicial review. The Minorities Ombudsman said that, although the Police Commissioner can accept or refuse a decision from the Complaints Body, "in practice, he "neglects" 90 percent of the Complaints Body's decisions. In a March 2011 ERRC report on anti-Roma violence in Hungary, which looked at 22 cases of violence against the Roma, the organization noted that police misconduct and procedural errors were documented during the investigation of one of the violent crimes against Roma, as raised by NGOs and later confirmed by the Independent Police Complaint Body and by the Head of Police.36
[23] The Minorities Ombudsman indicated that there is a general lack of information about appropriate forums through which victims of human rights violations can submit their complaints. He further stated that the main mechanisms for submitting human rights violations are only located in Budapest. For many settlements, the only available mechanism is something called a "minor-offence authority", which can be local municipal clerks, the police, or tax office. These authorities deal with minor "unlawful behaviour", such as refusal to provide services or the delivery of poor service in retail outlets, and their decisions can be contested in court. In addition, the Minorities Ombudsman pointed out that the majority of the Roma live in poor towns or small villages in rural areas of Hungary, making it difficult for them to find legal aid, whether from legal experts or NGOs that are working in the cities. The HHC notes that there are four ombudsmen in Hungary, two of which are the General Ombudsman and the Minorities Ombudsman. According to the Minorities Ombudsman, both he and the General Ombudsman, accept complaints of "maladministration", which includes racist or discriminatory behaviour, by public administrators or service suppliers. Although the decisions they make are not binding on public services providers or public authorities, their investigations "encourage consensus or mediation" and exert pressure for public policy changes. On discovering an infringement of constitutional rights, the Ombudsman proposes a resolution to the supervisory body of the entity which committed the violation. Should the proposal be rejected, the Ombudsman decides within 15 days upon amending, upholding or repealing the proposal. If the violating entity may be able to terminate the infringement itself, the Ombudsman initiates the remedy of the violation with the head of the given authority. The concerned authority shall respond within 30 days. If the initiative is rejected, the authority shall inform its supervisory body, which must respond to the Ombudsman within 30 days. The Ombudsman may also initiate that the public prosecutor file a warning with the concerned authority. The Ombudsman may initiate disciplinary proceedings with the competent body where he/she deems it necessary; in the case of a perceived criminal offence however he/she is obliged to initiate criminal proceedings. The Minorities Ombudsman receives 800 to 1,000 complaints annually. In 2010, 75 percent of those complaints related to the Roma and included charges of racial discrimination and unequal treatment. The HHC added that, in 2009, out of 1,012 complaints, 69 complained about the police, 34 the penitentiaries, and 54 the courts; in 2010, out of 1,064 complaints, 73 were police-related, 34 penitentiary-related, and 45 court-related. According to the HHC, the annual report on the Minorities Ombudsman's activities indicates that in 2009, out of 1,012 complaints, 603 (61%) involved the Roma, and in 2010, 739 (70%) of the 1,064 complaints were Roma-related. The Equal Treatment Authority (ETA), operating under the Ministry of Justice and Public Administration, is located in Budapest. The ETA receives complaints of discrimination based on race, religion, nationality, and health conditions, and it has the authority to impose fines. According to the Hungary report to the UN, the ETA can initiate proceedings upon individual complaints; that is, it conducts independent investigations based on individual complaints. Its authority extends to all actions and omissions of all public actors and a limited circle of private persons. If the right to equal treatment is violated, the ETA can impose sanctions. For example, it may order the elimination of the situation concerned, prohibit further continuation of the conduct concerned; publish its decision or impose a fine. The Minorities Ombudsman indicates that, in 2010, the ETA received 1,300 complaints and started investigations into 377 of them. Discrimination was determined to have taken place in 40 cases. Most of the cases were complaints of employment-related discrimination, but there were also instances of racism in the public service.37
[24] With regard to the activities of the Hungarian Guard in Hungary, and the Government's response to their actions, several political parties have condemned the Hungarian Guard, labelling it as "fascist". The National Roma Council demanded that it be banned along with other paramilitary groups after the Guard marched several times outside Roma villages to protest "Gypsy criminality". Various sources report that the Hungarian Guard was banned in 2009 by the Hungarian courts for rejecting equal rights to Romas and inciting resentment against them. However, the group reorganized itself into new organizations. According to multiple sources, the Hungarian Guard and other far-right organizations held demonstrations across the country with the goal of inciting prejudice against the Roma. There were also reports of anti-Roma marches by members of the Jobbik party, as well as the Hungarian Guard, that were held in rural towns to intimidate Romas. In Gyongyospata, several right-wing vigilante groups showed up claiming that local police are unable to protect white Hungarians from "Gypsy crime". According to the Parliamentary Commissioner for National and Ethnic Minority Rights, these "civil guardian" patrols have been frightening the minority community of Roma.38
[25] Sources report that incidences of violence against the Roma continued in 2010 and 2011. For example, sources report the use of Molotov cocktails in Siofok in 2010. The ERRC, which has documented the series of attacks against the Roma or their property, says that between January 2008 and July 2011, nine people have died and dozens were seriously injured. Various sources report on the tension between Roma and non-Roma residents in the village of Gyongyospata in 2011. Hungary's minority Ombudsman says that, because of its small and ineffective police force, political forces selected the village as an ideal location for increasing dissatisfaction over the police's efficiency and provoking conflict between Roma and non-Roma inhabitants. The Civil Guard Association for a Better Future was then invited to patrol the village, ostensibly to execute a mission of law and order. In March 2011, the Civil Guard began to patrol the streets and according to various sources, to intimidate Romani residents. More than 2,000 Jobbik party members and sympathizers also marched through the village on March 6, 2011. Later, around 200 vigilantes from the New Hungarian Guard, Civil Guard Association for a Better Future and Vagabonds for Protection of Hungarians marched on the streets. According to an AI action appeal, the police did nothing to prevent the harassment of Roma people by these groups, although four people were arrested on March 16, 2011 and police checkpoints were eventually established. In April of 2011, another far-right paramilitary group, Defence Force, organized a military training camp near Gyongyospata's Roma neighbourhood. According to media reports, on April 22, 2011, the Hungarian Red Cross evacuated approximately 270 Roma women and children from the village. The Hungarian English-language business news service, MTI-EcoNews, notes that the police dissolved the training camp following a government decree that imposes stricter punishments to uniformed people that organize unauthorized patrols. According to the Budapest Times, the Defence Force commander, Tamas Eszes, was detained along with some of his associates, but all were subsequently released after a court found no grounds to detain them. The Minorities Ombudsman states that his investigation into the incidents in Gyongyospata revealed several legislative and law enforcement deficiencies. He also requested the Prosecutor of Bekes County to examine the legal activity and conditions of the Society of Civil Guards for a Brighter Future and initiated the suspension of its activities by court. Sources indicate that in July 2011 a candidate of the Jobbik party was elected as mayor of Gyongyospata. According to the Budapest Times, the newly elected mayor announced that "he intends to set up a local gendarmerie to keep order in the town that has been plagued by politically fuelled ethnic tension in recent months.39
[26] The Hungarian government proposed changes to the Penal Code in 2011, in part to deal with the spate of street marches by groups of people dressed in "non-official uniforms". In May of 2011, the Hungarian Parliament adopted an amendment to the Criminal Code to protect the Roma community from "extreme right-wing groups. The amendment, which was a response to events involving right-wing groups dressed in uniforms, reportedly extends hate crimes legislation to include intimidation of ethnic, racial and religious or other groups.40
[27] Additional documentary evidence dealing with the police response in Hungary to racially motivated threats of violence or actual violence against the members of the Roma community, indicates that in 2009, police doubled the size of a task force investigating anti-Roma attacks. In July of 2009, police banned a demonstration that was to be held on Szabadsag Square in Budapest. The police indicated that they would "take firm action against any private individual or member of any organization, with special regard to the Hungarian Guard, who want to demonstrate. In August of 2009, police initiated legal proceedings against 16 members of the Hungarian Guard for "violating the ban on participation in any event of a legally disbanded civil organization. The Hungarian Guard members stood in front of the Budapest police headquarters while the Guard's founder, Gabo Vona, was in a hearing inside the building. Police had accused Vona of being the chief organizer of a ceremony to induct new members into the group that was held the week before. On April 20, 2010, charges were filed with the public prosecutor against the chief of the Hungarian Guard and two of his associates on "suspicion of illegally maintaining the uniformed organization". On the same day, police filed charges against three leaders of the Hungarian Guard. Because the group was still operating in spite of its ban, the charges also included "incitement against authorities decrees. Nine days later, the police filed charges against 10 leaders of the banned Hungarian Guard for violating the laws of assembly. The Deputy-Prosecutor-General said that the charges were raised because of an October 2009 demonstration Guard Members had attended in Debrecen in eastern Hungary. In July 2010, police arrested more than 70 members of the Hungarian National Guard at demonstrations in Budapest that were attended by around 600 members of the organization to commemorate the banning of the Hungarian Guard. Police also arrested another 20 Hungarian National Guard members attending the same rallies because they were in possession of minor firearms. During an April 2011 anti-Roma rally, at which 600 Jobbik supporters demonstrated against "Gypsy terror" in Hejoszalonta, police in riot gear installed metal barriers to "keep the Jobbik supporters away from a counter-rally by human rights groups".41
[28] Based on my review of the totality of the documentary evidence with respect to the human rights situation of the Romani minority community in Hungary, both in the past and the present, I acknowledge and accept that the preponderance of the documentary evidence establishes that, despite the substantial efforts made by the Hungarian government to improve the socio-economic status of the Roma, which includes passing comprehensive anti-discrimination and equal treatment legislation, adopting new measures aimed at creating employment and educational opportunities, creating new government departments and agencies tasked with the responsibility of monitoring, promoting and defending the rights of Roma, and appropriating substantial sums of money to programs designed to create new opportunities for the Roma, there continues to be significant and widespread discrimination against the Roma community in the areas of education, employment, housing, social services, and health care. However, I find that there is no persuasive evidence before me to establish that the discrimination which Roma face in Hungary, at the present time, in these different areas, either individually or cumulatively, rise to a level of serious harm amounting to persecution or a risk to life, a risk of torture, or a risk of cruel and unusual treatment or punishment.
[29] Based on my review of the extensive documentary evidence which forms part of the evidentiary record in this case, I acknowledge and accept that there have been, and continue to be, reported cases of racially-motivated threats and violent attacks perpetrated by racist and extremist individuals and groups against members of the Roma community in Hungary. I acknowledge and accept that there have been recent reports indicating that there has been an increase in violent rhetoric and hate speeches delivered by members of these racist groups, certain segments of the Hungarian media, by some local politicians, which are stoking the flames of racial intolerance and prejudice towards the Roma. Furthermore, I also acknowledge that recent media reports indicate that that there have been an increase in anti-Roma marches and demonstrations carried out by extremist groups whose purpose is to antagonize and intimidate members of the Romani community and mobilize public support for their cause. However, I find that there is no persuasive evidence to support a conclusion that members of the Romani community, in the social and economic climate prevailing in Hungary at the present time, are being subjected to widespread and systematic acts of racially motivated violence and threats of violence on a large scale affecting the entire Roma population throughout the country.
[30] Based on my review of the documentary evidence as a whole, additionally, I acknowledge and accept that prejudice and negative attitudes towards the Roma continue to be problems affecting the day to day operations of the police forces in Hungary. When it comes to the level and degree of effectiveness of policing operations in response to racially motivated attacked and serious crimes committed by neo-Nazi groups, skinheads, and other extremist and nationalistic groups operating in Hungary, such as the Hungarian Guard, I find that the preponderance of the evidence demonstrates that, although police services and policing operations carried out in response to crimes committed against the Roma community are not always effective and have been deficient from time to time, in the majority of cases, the Hungarian police services are able and willing to provide effective protection in practice to Roma who have become targets of racially motivated violent crime. Furthermore, I find that the preponderance of the documentary evidence on current country conditions in Hungary does not support the conclusion that there has been a systematic failure on the part of Hungarian law enforcement agencies to deliver responsible and effective protection to members of the Roma community in practice. On the contrary, I find that the preponderance of the documentary evidence establishes that police authorities in Hungary in the majority of cases do take crimes committed against the Roma community seriously, they do investigate these crimes, they do arrest perpetrators, they do lay charges against perpetrators, and they do refer matters to the courts and Public Prosecutors so that these criminals can be put on trial and brought to justice. I also find that the preponderance of the documentary evidence establishes that, where there are shortcomings with respect to police investigations into violent crimes committed against the Roma or there has been some wrongdoing or misconduct directed at a Roma victim of crime by a police officer, there are, in practice, effective mechanisms and remedies available to the Roma who have become victims and targets of racially motivated violence. According to the documentary evidence, where there is police misconduct or maladministration in terms of the handling of a complaint from a member of the Roma community or in conducting a proper investigation into the complaint, an aggrieved party can make a complaint to the local police unit supervisor who is required to make an administrative decision regarding the decision that was made by a police officer in the first instance when responding to a complaint from a Roma. The decision of this supervisor can be challenged in the court. An aggrieved party can also make a complaint to National Police headquarters in Budapest. Although a complaint can also be made to an Independent Police Complaints Body, which is tasked with the responsibility of investigating complaints made against police officers in discharging their public duties, I find this oversight mechanism is not particularly effective because, even where the independent police body finds that there has been wrongdoing by the police force and makes a recommendation to the Police Commissioner or Police chief, the Police Commissioner can ignore or reject the recommendations being made by the Police Complaints Body. According to the documentary evidence, in practice, the Police Commissioner ignores 90 % of the recommendations made by the Independent Police Complaints Body. On the other hand, I find that there are two other mechanisms available to Roma victims of violence which are effective means by which they can obtain redress for police mishandling of their complaint or police ineptitude or police indifference and discriminatory treatment in dealing with their complaint. One is the Parliamentary Minority Rights Commissioner, otherwise known as Minority Ombudsman. The Minority Ombudsman has very broad powers of investigation and can commence investigations on his or her own initiative concerning police misconduct and can even initiate civil or criminal prosecutions in the courts where warranted by the evidence. Moreover, although the Minority Ombudsman cannot force a public authority such as the police force to adopt his or her recommendations, the documentary evidence indicates that there is a high acceptance rate for the recommendations that he or she makes with regard to the resolution of a complaint against the police. Another state agency which, according to the documentary evidence, has broad powers to investigate, sanction, and even fine public authorities that do not discharge their duties and responsibilities in accordance with the law, including police officers, is the Equal Treatment Authority in Hungary.
[31] The preponderance of the objective evidence regarding current country conditions suggests that, although not perfect, adequate state protection is available in practice to victims of crime in Hungary, including crimes committed against the Roma, that Hungary is making serious efforts to address the problems of criminality, and that the police are both willing and able to protect victims. Police corruption and deficiencies, although existing and noted, are not systemic. I am of the view in canvassing the documentary evidence, that, as a whole, the issues of corruption and deficiencies are being addressed by the state of Hungary.
[32] The female claimant testified on behalf of all of the claimants. She was questioned as to what efforts she made to seek state protection before fleeing Hungary. The female claimant provided the following particulars with respect to the incidents that she and her family experienced in Hungary on account of her Romani ethnicity and the efforts that she made to engage the assistance and protection of the Hungarian police and the authorities and the manner in which they responded.
[33] The female claimant's parents are still residing in Hungary. The last time the female claimant spoke to them was in the summer of 2010. At that time, her mother was working and her father remained indoors and was afraid to go outside because he feared that if he went outside he would be hurt the way his children and grandchildren were hurt. The female claimant also has a sister who continues to reside in Hungary. The last time she communicated with her was around 3 to 4 years ago. Her sister is married and has two children. When the claimant last spoke to her 3 to 4 years ago, her sister was not having any problems because she has very light skin tone and she is blond. The female claimant's mother was often confronted by members of the Hungarian Guard who wanted to know about the whereabouts of the female claimant and her family.
[34] The female claimant's 3 children all had problems with the Hungarian Guard. The 2 youngest sons were threatened and the oldest son was beaten up by the Guards. The Guards threatened the female claimant's youngest sons by telling them that they were going to cut their throats unless they left Hungary.
[35] The female claimant has a brother who is currently residing in Canada. In XXXX of 2008, he left Hungary and came to Canada where he made a refugee claim. The female claimant's brother was called a dirty gypsy and then subsequently attacked with a hammer by his Hungarian neighbour in 2001. The Hungarian neighbour ended up calling the police. When the police arrived, they blamed the female claimant's brother for the attack. The female claimant and her husband were present at the time of the attack of her brother. She told the police that it was the Hungarian neighbour who attacked her brother. When the female claimant said this to the police they laughed at her. The attacker was not charged by the police and he simply walked away from the incident. Considering the very limited information and details which the female claimant provided with respect to the surrounding circumstances giving rise to the attack and the conduct of the police in response to the call by the Hungarian neighbour, I cannot find that the female claimant is similarly situated to her brother. The female claimant's brother was also attacked by the Guards on the street in 2008. The Guards beat him up very badly and told him that if he and his family did not leave Hungary they would kill his entire family. After the female claimant's brother was attacked by the Guards, he reported the incident to the police. The police told him that they could not help him because by the time the police arrived the perpetrators of the attack had fled. I find that the female claimant has furnished no persuasive evidence to show that that police did absolutely nothing to investigate, locate, or apprehend the perpetrators of the attack on her brother. Given the lack of detailed information provided by the female claimant with respect to the surrounding circumstances giving rise to her brother's attack in 2008 and his reporting of the incident to the police and their response to his complaint, I cannot find that her brother is similarly situated to her.
[36] In the summer of 2004, while the female claimant was at home alone with her 3 sons, 2 men unknown men broke into her home. Once they were inside, they wanted to know the whereabouts of the female claimant's brother. She did not tell them where her brother was. Because she did not cooperate, the two men pushed her off her balcony which was on the ground floor of her home. As a result, she injured her right ankle. The 2 men did not tell the female claimant why they were looking for her brother. The 2 men did not do anything to the female claimant's children. Before the 2 men left the female claimant's home, they warned her not to go and report the incident to the police or else she would have more serious problems. After receiving this warning, the female claimant decided not to pursue a complaint with the police. I find in these circumstances, given that these 2 men invaded the female claimant's privacy and entered her home and threatened her and assaulted her, in the company of her 3 sons, that it was objectively unreasonable for the female claimant to have not taken any steps whatsoever to report this incident to the Hungarian police. I find that the female claimant has not furnished any persuasive evidence to show that had she gone to the police in 2004 after she was attacked that the police would have been unresponsive or would not have investigated her complaint or tried to look for the perpetrators who attacked her in the privacy of her own home.
[37] In 2005, the home of the female claimant's brother was firebombed. A Molotov cocktail was thrown at the front door of his home and it caught fire. The female claimant's brother was not home, nor was his family at the time of the attack. Her brother worked in XXXXXX. He had lent a large sum of money to some people. He approached these people and asked them several times for his money back. These people who owed her brother the money got angry and they threatened to harm him personally and warned him that there would be reprisals against his property. It was after this threat was made that the female claimant's brother's house was attacked. The female claimant did not know the people to whom her brother had lent the money. On her way home, the female claimant received a call from one of her brother's neighbours who told her that her brother's home had been attacked. The female claimant called the fire department and the police and then headed to her brother's house. When she got there, the police and the fire department were already there. An ambulance was also there. When the female claimant arrived, the police asked her questions about what had happened. The police wanted to know where her brother was and asked the female claimant if she had any idea as to who was responsible for the attack. The female claimant's husband told the police that her brother had been threatened before this attack on his home had taken place but the police did not believe her. The female claimant told the police that the reason her brother's house was firebombed was because he was a Roma. The female claimant gave contradictory testimony during her testimony. At first, she said that the police indicated to her that they were going to look for the people who were responsible for the attack and that they were going to find them that very same night. When she was questioned by her counsel about the same incident, she said that when she told the police that her brother's house was firebombed because he was a Roma, the police then told the female claimant that they could not help them because they were Roma. When she was asked to clarify by her counsel what the police actually said to her, she said that they could not really help them because they were Roma and indicated to her that most likely the case would be closed because the perpetrators were unknown. When the female claimant was questioned again by her counsel about whether the police had indicated to her that they would investigate the firebombing of her brother's house, she said that the police told her that they were going to conduct an investigation and would call the female claimant and her husband to the police station to have them sign a police report with respect to the attack on her brother's house. The female claimant added that no one from the police station contacted her or told her to come into the police station to sign the report. The female claimant also testified in response to her counsel's questions that she did not provide the police with the name or address of the perpetrator whom she believed was behind the attack on her brother's home. The female claimant did not know the name and address of the perpetrator, only that the person responsible was a person who owed her brother a substantial sum of money. Counsel asked the female claimant if she knew whether the police carried out an investigation. She said that she did not know because she did not receive any report from the police when her brother's house was attacked or following the attack. The police did not find the people who carried out the attack on her brother's home. The female claimant believed that her brother's neighbours were not questioned about the attack, that only she and her husband were questioned. Counsel asked the male claimant if he was at the scene of the firebombing of his brother-in-law's house. He said that he was. Counsel asked if the police questioned him about the incident. He said that they did not really ask him any questions, but that he volunteered information to the police but that they did not really listen or pay attention to what he said. I find that the female claimant has not furnished any persuasive evidence to show that the Hungarian police did not conduct a proper or thorough investigation into the attack on her brother's house or that they did not make any efforts to locate the people who were responsible for the attack. Furthermore, I find that the female claimant did not furnish any persuasive evidence to show that the police did not question any potential eye witnesses who lived in the neighbourhood either on the day of the attack or following the attack. I find, given the very limited information and details provided by the female claimant concerning the circumstances surrounding the firebombing of her brother's house and the police response to the call for help, that I cannot conclude that the female claimant was similarly situated to her brother.
[38] In XXXXXX of 2006, the female claimant's husband was assaulted by one of his coworkers because his coworker, who was Hungarian, hated gypsies. After the attack, the female claimant and her husband went to the police station to report the incident. At the police station, the female claimant's husband was questioned about what happened at his work. The female claimant and her husband produced a medical report regarding her husband's injuries. Counsel asked the female claimant if she told the police that her husband was attacked because he was half Roma. She said that she told police this but that they were not interested in hearing this statement. They identified the perpetrator to the police by his name. When the female claimant was questioned first about this incident, she said that the police did not prepare a written report. Later, when her counsel asked her if she or her husband told the clinic where her husband went to get treated that the attack was racially motivated, the female claimant testified that when she and her husband arrived at the clinic, the clinic already had a police report in their possession. The female claimant spoke to her husband's boss and his boss told her that the police did not attend at the work place to question him about what had happened to her husband. The police did not arrest her husband's coworker but they sent him a letter. The female claimant did have personal knowledge of the contents of this letter. Her husband's coworker showed this letter to her husband and laughed at him telling him that police did nothing to him. As far as the female claimant knows, the perpetrator who attacked her husband at work continued to work at the company after he assaulted her husband and he continues to work there up until the present time. Counsel asked the male claimant if following the attack by his coworker he told the police that his coworker attacked him because he was Roma and that this coworker had been harassing him in the past. The male claimant confirmed that that was the case. Counsel then asked the male claimant if the police paid attention to the fact that his coworker had been harassing him before in the past. The male claimant said that it made no difference to the police. I find that the female claimant furnished no persuasive evidence to suggest that she took any further steps to pursue the complaint made against her husband's coworker or to make an independent complaint against the police for their apparent negligence in conducting a thorough and proper investigation and for failing to apprehend a known suspect involved in the assault on her husband. I find that the female claimant did not furnish any persuasive evidence to show that had she taken additional steps to complain about the police misconduct or dereliction of duty that no meaningful or effective action would have been taken by the police against the coworker of the female claimant's husband.
[39] The female claimant's older son was beaten up by Hungarian Guards in XXXX of 2007. Neither the female claimant nor her husband reported this incident to the police. They did not report it to the police because the Guards threatened her oldest son and her two other younger sons. Given the gravity of the situation, the assault on her oldest son by the Guards and their threats to beat up the oldest son again along with her younger two sons, I find, in the circumstances, that it was objectively unreasonable for the female claimant and her husband to have taken no steps whatsoever to contact the police to make a report and ask them for help. I find that the female claimant did not furnish any persuasive evidence to show that had she and her husband reported the incident and the serious threats of violence made against her children that police authorities would not have taken any effective steps to investigate and locate the perpetrators involved in the incident.
[40] In XXX of 2009, the female claimant began to receive anonymous threatening phone calls on her personal cell phone. The caller warned the female claimant and her husband that if they did not leave the country that they would cut their throats. The female claimant's husband also received basically the same anonymous threatening phone calls. Neither the female claimant nor her husband reported these threatening phone calls to the police. They did not report the threatening phone calls to the police because they felt that the police would not help them in any event. They did not believe that the police would take any action because previously when her brother's house was firebombed and her husband was beaten up the police did not do anything. I find, given the seriousness of the multiple threats made against the female claimant and her husband to cut their throats unless they left Hungary, that the subjective reluctance of the female claimant and her husband to report the threats to police authorities was objectively unreasonable. I find that the female claimant has not furnished any persuasive evidence to show that in XXX of 2009 there was a systematic failure on the part of the Hungarian police to offer Roma facing serious threats of violence any effective or meaningful protection in practice.
[41] In XXXX of 2009, at night, the Guards came to the female claimant's home when she was at home alone with her children. Several men broke into the house and one man stayed outside of the house in the garden. The men were wearing uniforms of the Hungarian Guard. The men who entered the home approached the female claimant and wanted to know the whereabouts of her brother and her husband. The female claimant refused to cooperate with them or tell them where they were and told the men to leave her alone. One of the men held her arms and another man slapped her in the face, yelled at her, pulled her hair, and called her a dirty gypsy slut. The female claimant's oldest son heard the commotion and he came downstairs only to be confronted and beaten up by the Guards. After the female claimant and her oldest son were beaten up, she did not report the incident to the police. Counsel asked the male claimant why this incident was not reported to the police. He said that it was not reported because the police had not helped him and his wife in the past and the Guards had threatened to harm the male claimant if he reported it to the police. The female claimant did not report the incident to the police because she did not have the courage to do so and she and her son were in hiding. I find that the subjective reluctance of the female claimant and her husband to report what happened, despite the threat from the Guards, is not a legitimate excuse or valid reason for her failure to approach police authorities for help and assistance. I find that the female claimant did not furnish any persuasive evidence to show that had she reported the beating which she and her oldest son had suffered at the hands of the Hungarian Guard that police authorities would not have taken any effective measures to investigate the attack on the female claimant and her son or to find and locate and arrest the perpetrators of this crime.
[42] I find, in the particular circumstances of this case, that the female claimant did not make diligent efforts nor did she take all reasonable steps in seeking the protection of the Hungarian police and law enforcement authorities prior to leaving Hungary and coming to Canada. Therefore, I find that the female claimant has not rebutted the presumption of adequate state protection in Hungary with clear and convincing evidence and I am not persuaded to believe that, if the female claimant were to return to Hungary today, that neither effective nor adequate state protection would reasonably be made available to her in practice, if she were to seek it.
[43] Therefore, I find that the female claimant is not a Convention refugee and her claim under section 96 of the IRPA fails. The rest of the analysis of this decision will be under section 97 of the IRPA.
Section 97 - Persons in Need of Protection
[44] For the same reasons as outlined above, I find that the female claimant would not face, on a balance of probabilities, a risk to life, or risk of torture, or risk of cruel and unusual treatment or punishment in Hungary if she were to return there today.
[45] Therefore, I find the female claimant is not a person in need of protection and the claim under section 97 of the IRPA fails.
Claim of Male Claimant and the Children
[46] Since the claim of the male claimant and his children is largely dependent upon the claim of the female claimant, and there is no persuasive evidence to significantly differentiate their claims from hers, for the same reasons as above, I find that the male claimant and his children are neither Convention refugees nor persons in need of protection and that they would not face a serious possibility of persecution for a Convention reason and would not face, on a balance of probabilities, a risk to life or risk of torture or risk of cruel and unusual treatment or punishment in Hungary if they were to return there today.
CONCLUSION
[47] I determine that the claimants are neither Convention refugees nor persons in need of protection. The Refugee Protection Division therefore rejects their claims.
(signed)
"Robert Bafaro"
Robert Bafaro
April 30, 2012
Date
